---
title: "Citizenship"
description: "Canadian-born, naturalized citizens, and non-citizens, mapped by census tract — 2021 Census"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os, json
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import choropleth_groups_map, peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar
```

## Citizenship & nativity, by neighbourhood

Where the **Canadian-born**, **naturalized citizens** (immigrants who have become
citizens), and **non-citizens** live, by **census tract** (2021). **Use the
dropdown to switch group**, and zoom and pan to your area. The pattern is the
geography of arrival: newcomer neighbourhoods show high non-citizen shares,
longer-settled immigrant neighbourhoods show high naturalized shares, and much of
the rest of the urban map is 80–95% Canadian-born.

**How the groups are assembled.** The census reports *citizenship* (citizen or
not) and *immigrant status* (non-immigrant / immigrant / non-permanent resident)
on the same population base, and we combine them transparently:

- **Canadian-born** — StatCan's "non-immigrants" (Canadian citizens by birth).
- **Naturalized citizens** — Canadian citizens *minus* the Canadian-born.
- **Non-citizens** — direct from the census (permanent residents on track to citizenship, temporary residents, and others).

The three groups sum to 100%.
**Non-permanent residents** — people on work or study permits or claiming asylum,
central to the recent [population-growth story](index.qmd) — are a *subset* of
non-citizens, shown as a fourth map option. Census citizenship questions are
asked of a 25% sample, so small tracts carry more noise than the diversity and
language layers.

```{python}
#| echo: false
geo_path = "data/geo/ct_2021.geojson"
cit_path = "data/geo/statcan_ct_citizenship_2021.csv"
CIT_GROUPS = [
    ("canadian_born", "Canadian-born"),
    ("naturalized", "Naturalized citizens"),
    ("non_citizens", "Non-citizens"),
    ("npr", "Non-permanent residents"),
]
if os.path.exists(geo_path) and os.path.exists(cit_path):
    geo = json.load(open(geo_path))
    cit = pd.read_csv(cit_path, dtype={"ctuid": str})
    fig = choropleth_groups_map(geo, cit, "ctuid", CIT_GROUPS, name_col="name",
        pop_col="population", colorscale="Viridis", opacity=0.6,
        breakdown_exclude=["npr"],
        source_note="Source: Statistics Canada, 2021 Census Profile 98-401-X2021007.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Citizenship map not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## Becoming a citizen: how Canada compares

The map shows who *is* a citizen; this shows how many people *become* citizens each year. Counting acquisitions of citizenship per 100,000 residents puts countries of very different sizes on the same footing. Canada naturalises new citizens at one of the highest rates in the peer group — typically alongside Australia and New Zealand, and far above the United States, France, and (lowest) Japan. The Canadian line is volatile because it tracks **processing throughput** as much as demand: grants fell sharply in 2020 when offices closed, then surged in 2022–23 as the pandemic backlog was cleared. Countries also count acquisitions differently (some include registration and declaration routes that others exclude), so this is best read as broad magnitudes, not an exact ranking.

```{python}
#| echo: false
nat_path = "data/population/oecd_naturalisations.csv"
if os.path.exists(nat_path):
    nat = pd.read_csv(nat_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(nat, "year", "nat_per_100k",
        title="", yaxis_title="Naturalisations per 100,000 residents",
        show_average=True, initial_start=2000,
        source_note=f"Source: OECD International Migration Database (acquisitions of nationality); per 100k via World Bank population | Data as of: {get_data_date(nat_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Naturalisation data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

In the most recent year, ranked:

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(nat_path):
    fig = ranked_bar(nat, "nat_per_100k", "Naturalisations per 100,000 residents",
        f"Source: OECD International Migration Database (acquisitions of nationality); per 100k via World Bank population | Data as of: {get_data_date(nat_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Voting

Citizenship confers the right to vote. Whether Canadians exercise it has moved over more than 150 years of federal elections — drifting between roughly 60% and 80% of registered electors, with a mid-20th-century peak, a low in 2008, and a partial recovery since.

```{python}
#| echo: false
to_path = "data/population/voter_turnout.csv"
if os.path.exists(to_path):
    to = pd.read_csv(to_path)
    fig = go.Figure(go.Scatter(x=to["year"], y=to["turnout"], mode="lines+markers",
        line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=2), marker=dict(size=5),
        hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:.1f}% turnout<extra></extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=440,
        yaxis=dict(title="Turnout (% of electors)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        margin=dict(t=30, b=120),
        annotations=[dict(text=f"Source: Elections Canada (federal general elections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(to_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.30, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Voter-turnout data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

Turnout also climbs steeply with age. Adults under 35 vote at the lowest rates and Canadians in their late 60s and early 70s at the highest, with a slight dip among the oldest — a gap that has persisted across recent elections.

```{python}
#| echo: false
age_path = "data/population/voter_turnout_by_age.csv"
if os.path.exists(age_path):
    ag = pd.read_csv(age_path)
    latest_yr = int(ag["year"].max())
    cur = ag[ag["year"] == latest_yr].sort_values("age_group")
    SHORT = {"18 to 24 years": "18–24", "25 to 34 years": "25–34", "35 to 44 years": "35–44",
             "45 to 54 years": "45–54", "55 to 64 years": "55–64", "65 to 74 years": "65–74",
             "75 years and over": "75+"}
    fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(x=cur["age_group"].map(SHORT), y=cur["turnout"],
        marker_color="#4e79a7", text=[f"{v:.0f}%" for v in cur["turnout"]], textposition="outside",
        hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:.1f}% turnout<extra></extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=400,
        yaxis=dict(title="Turnout (%)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%", range=[0, 88]),
        xaxis=dict(title="Age group"), margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
        annotations=[dict(text=f"Source: Elections Canada — estimated turnout by age, {latest_yr} federal election.",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.2, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Turnout-by-age data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Download data

- [Citizenship & Nativity by Census Tract (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_ct_citizenship_2021.csv) — 2021 Census of Population, Census Profile 98-401-X2021007
- [Naturalisations per 100k, Canada vs OECD peers, 1995– (CSV)](../data/population/oecd_naturalisations.csv) — OECD International Migration Database + World Bank population
- [Federal voter turnout, 1867– (CSV)](../data/population/voter_turnout.csv) — Elections Canada
- [Voter turnout by age, 2004– (CSV)](../data/population/voter_turnout_by_age.csv) — Elections Canada
